data.title: Holcim Acquires Peru's Compañía Minera Luren
data.description:
Swiss building materials company Holcim acquired Peru-based Compañía Minera Luren.

The acquired company provides walling systems and other specialty building materials, according to a Thursday release. Holcim said the acquisition is highly complementary to its existing business in Peru.

data.title: Aditya Birla Housing Finance to issue over 3-year bonds, bankers say
data.description:
India's Aditya Birla Housing Finance plans to raise 10 billion rupees ($116.8 million), including a greenshoe option of 7 billion rupees, through the sale of bonds maturing in three years and five months, three bankers said on Thursday.

The company will pay an annual coupon of 7.8618% and has invited bids from bankers and investors on Friday, they said.

The company did not reply to a Reuters email for comment.

data.title: U.S. Treasury Yields Slide as Tariffs Raise Recession FearsMarket Talk
data.description:
0534 GMT - U.S. Treasury prices rallied in the wake of President Trump's tariff announcements, with yields sliding across maturities, driven by the five- to 10-year maturities, according to Tradeweb data. "This morning global bond yields are declining as the tariffs are seen to be much more aggressive than expected and raising the fear of a recession," says Danske Bank Research's chief analyst Stefan Mellin in a note. The two-year Treasury yield falls 10.5 basis points to last trade at 3.799%; the five-year Treasury yield falls 14.5 basis points to 3.807%; the 10-year Treasury yield slides almost 14 basis points to 4.059%. The 30-year Treasury yield drops 10.5 basis points to 4.446%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0530 GMT - China is likely to escalate its retaliation for the latest U.S. tariffs, although that will inevitably come with supply chain disruptions and further strains on trade relations, Pepperstone research strategist Dilin Wu writes in a note. As tensions escalate, China will need to strengthen ties with alternative trading partners and step up stimulus measures for domestic consumption, Wu adds. If Chinese manufacturers can seize new opportunities and China deepens trade cooperation with the EU and other Asian economies, some related assets could emerge more resilient amid the shifting global trade landscape, Wu says. Investors are closely watching for Beijing's next moves with China's current counter-tariffs set at a relatively moderate 10% to 15%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0528 GMT - Swedish Prime Minister Ulf Kristersson said U.S tariffs will make Americans and Swedes poorer and the world more dangerous. Mutual cooperation through free trade has contributed to peace, technological progress and reduced poverty globally, he says. "It is free enterprise and free competition that have laid the foundation for the prosperity of the west. That is why Americans can listen to music on Swedish Spotify and we Swedes can listen to the same music on American iPhones." However, Sweden is well prepared for what is happening, with a stable economic footing and "world-class" government finances, he adds. "We want to find a way together with the U.S. where we trade and cooperate so that people in our countries are better off." (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0523 GMT - Trade organization spiritsEurope says it supports any efforts made by the European Union to reach a negotiated solution with the U.S. as the spirits sector braces for the tariffs announced by President Trump. The industry is highly integrated across the Atlantic, spiritsEurope says, making the sector particularly vulnerable to tariffs. The association, which represents the sector in the EU, says it is prepared to contribute with a proposal to improve the fortunes of the industry on both sides of the Atlantic."We want to ensure a return to an environment that supports continued growth for both EU and U.S. producers," the association adds. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0521 GMT - U.S. President Trump's reciprocal tariffs make central banks' interest-rate cuts difficult, says Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. The tariffs announced Wednesday are "the punchier/most bearish end of expectations, with high levies, and little time to negotiate them away before implementation," the senior research strategist says. "Good luck getting any rate cuts amid the macro mess that lot will cause!" Brown says that 'Liberation Day' seems much more likely to be the beginning of a tit-for-tat trade war across the globe, rather than a clearing event that brings the issue of tariffs to an end from a market and macro perspective. The U.S. will impose a 10% tariff on all imports and much higher rates for some nations, according to Trump's announcement. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0518 GMT - The latest tariffs announced by U.S. President Trump marks a sharp escalation of his trade war, Vasu Menon, OCBC managing director of investment strategy writes in a note. Although sectoral tariffs on copper, semiconductors, pharmaceuticals and lumber products were not in focus, they could still be hit with tariffs in coming months, he says. But the wait for Trump's biggest trade salvo--reciprocal tariffs--is over, he says. The anticipation of what Trump could do had caused a great deal of uncertainty, he notes. "Nevertheless, markets will need time to internalize and assess Trump's latest tariff bazooka and this along with the risk of retaliation from trading partners will keep investors on edge and nervous for now," he says.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0513 GMT - China is likely to start bolstering trade defenses ahead of negotiations, Macquarie analyst Eugene Hsiao writes in a note. Trump's new tariff announcements signal that now is the real starting point for trade negotiations, he says. While the potential shock to China's economy has already been widely discussed, markets remain in a holding pattern on discounting the real impact to growth until more clarity is reached, he says. China's recent tech re-rating is mostly insulated from tariffs, he says, adding that investment focus now returns to potential domestic stimulus to boost consumption to offset tariff impact. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0510 GMT - The Chinese yuan weakens against the dollar in offshore and onshore markets, dragged by U.S. President Trump's tariff announcement overnight. Emerging-market Asian currencies took a hit from the reciprocal tariffs, Mizuho Securities Asia's Ken Cheung says in an email. Several Asian countries such as Vietnam, Thailand, and Taiwan are facing significant reciprocal tariff increases, the director of FX Strategy says. "Looking ahead, we reckon that the PBOC will allow gradual CNY fixing weakening to introduce more two-way market flexibility to adjust to choppy market conditions after the US tariff day," Cheung adds. USD/CNY is 0.4% higher at 7.2983; USD/CNH is up 0.2% at 7.3114. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0504 GMT - Spain will keep fighting for an open world, premier Pedro Sanchez insists as the EU braces for 20% tariffs on its exports to the U.S., the bloc's most important trading partner. The trade duties, part of a major package set out Wednesday by President Trump, could hamstring exporters and depress Europe's growth. "Spain will protect its companies and its workers and will continue to back openness in the world," Sanchez says on X following a meeting with employer and worker associations.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0504 GMT - While Australia faces a 10% duty on sales to the U.S. under President Trump's sweeping tariff plan, tiny Norfolk Island--an external Australian territory--faces a tariff of 29%, a decision that's perplexing Australian officials. "We regard the Norfolk Island decision as a rather strange one," says Prime Minister Anthony Albanese. "It, of course, is a part of Australia" but "with a 29% tariff imposed." Australia's government will be seeking an explanation from the Trump administration, he says. "I'm not sure that Norfolk Island, with respect to it, is a threat to the United States economy, and therefore, I think it being singled out is rather strange," Albanese says. Norfolk Island had a population of 2,188 people in a 2021 census. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0502 GMT - Europe faces a recession risk after Donald Trump unveiled steep tariffs on imports from the EU and the U.K., economists at Barclays warn. The tariffs are higher than might have been expected, Barclays notes, in what marks a sharply new protectionist turn for global trade. That adds to existing uncertainty and limp growth across the EU and the U.K. "Tariffs announced on U.S. imports of EU and U.K. goods... dampen the economic outlook and put euro area economies and the U.K. at risk of a recession in the 2H 2025," Barclays warns clients in a note. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0447 GMT - Both Japan and South Korea will look to reduce Trump's reciprocal tariffs rather than seek to hit back at the respective 24% and 25% levies, ING's Min Joo Kang says. The marginal impact of the new tariffs is likely to be smaller than the separate 25% duty on autos, which is the largest category of exports to the U.S. for both countries, the economist says. Semiconductors and pharma--both major export items from Japan and Korea--have been spared, she notes, expecting the negative impact to be more concentrated in heavy industry and machinery. "Neither country is likely to retaliate," she adds, instead seeing them making concessions by importing more U.S. products. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

data.title: Trump Tariffs Likely to Provoke Fresh Easing at Central BanksMarket Talk
data.description: President Trump's tariffs are steeper than might have been expected and will probably spur new cuts to interest rates across the world's developed economies, Capital Economics' Neil Shearing and Paul Ashworth write in a note. A 10% baseline plus tailored duties on individual economies and goods-sectors will make up an effective U.S. tariff rate of around 25% all told, according to Capital's calculations. China will be hit the hardest, Shearing and Ashworth say. But decimal points will also be shaved off growth in the U.K and the eurozone, they say. "Most advanced-economy central banks will probably view this as another reason to cut interest rates," they add. The European Central Bank will decide its next policy steps later in April, and the Bank of England in May. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: No Escape For U.K. From Trade War, Business Leaders SayMarket Talk
data.description: The Trump administration's 10% tariff on U.S. imports of British goods, along with a 25% tariff on vehicles, dashes any hopes that the U.K. would avoid being involved in a trade war, the Institute of Directors says in a note. The tariffs represent another barrier to growth as they will further add pressures to U.K. companies, the organization of British business leaders says. Exporters will have to review the viability of the U.S. as a destination and as a supply chain location, or may have to cut their profit margins to remain competitive, the group adds. The U.S. is the U.K.'s largest trading partner and a key export market for the British industries, particularly the automotive, pharmaceutical, chemicals and whisky sectors, the IoD says. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0530 GMT - China is likely to escalate its retaliation for the latest U.S. tariffs, although that will inevitably come with supply chain disruptions and further strains on trade relations, Pepperstone research strategist Dilin Wu writes in a note. As tensions escalate, China will need to strengthen ties with alternative trading partners and step up stimulus measures for domestic consumption, Wu adds. If Chinese manufacturers can seize new opportunities and China deepens trade cooperation with the EU and other Asian economies, some related assets could emerge more resilient amid the shifting global trade landscape, Wu says. Investors are closely watching for Beijing's next moves with China's current counter-tariffs set at a relatively moderate 10% to 15%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0523 GMT - Trade organization spiritsEurope says it supports any efforts made by the European Union to reach a negotiated solution with the U.S. as the spirits sector braces for the tariffs announced by President Trump. The industry is highly integrated across the Atlantic, spiritsEurope says, making the sector particularly vulnerable to tariffs. The association, which represents the sector in the EU, says it is prepared to contribute with a proposal to improve the fortunes of the industry on both sides of the Atlantic."We want to ensure a return to an environment that supports continued growth for both EU and U.S. producers," the association adds. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0518 GMT - New U.S. tariffs mean U.K. car manufacturers might have to review their output in the face of constrained demand, U.K. industry body Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders said. U.K. products exported to the U.S. have been slapped with a 10% tariff, which is another deeply disappointing and potentially damaging measure, SMMT Chief Executive Mike Hawes says. British cars were already set to attract a 25% tariff and other automotive products are now set to be impacted immediately. "While we hope a deal between the U.K. and U.S. can still be negotiated, this is yet another challenge to a sector already facing multiple headwinds." The tariff costs can't be absorbed by manufacturers, so U.S. consumers may face additional costs and a reduced choice of British brands, he adds. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0510 GMT - China's export-driven companies will face challenges after President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs, either in the form of lower margins or reduced sales volumes if they pass on the additional costs to customers, Morningstar analyst Ivan Su says. Weaker exports could further slow China's economic growth and in turn dampen domestic consumption, he adds. "This broader economic slowdown could create a ripple effect, ultimately impacting even domestically-oriented companies," he adds. Su notes that the key factor is how much additional stimulus the Chinese government is prepared to implement to offset the tariff-induced economic slowdown. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Elekta and Azra AI announce partnership to enhance cancer registry operations with AI-powered automation
data.description:
Collaboration between Azra AI and Elekta will boost cancer registry workflow efficiency, reduce manual data entry, and set the stage for future advancements

SAN JOSE, Calif., – Elekta (EKTA-B.ST) announced today that it has partnered with Azra AI, the industry’s only end-to-end oncology workflow automation platform, to enhance cancer registry operations through automation and AI-powered data management.

This partnership combines Azra AI’s real-time patient identification and workflow automation with Elekta’s next-generation cancer registry software, Elekta ONE Registry Informatics*. The combined technologies will enable cancer registry teams to automate cancer casefinding, streamline data ingestion, increase reporting accuracy, and ensure compliance with national and international standards.

"Cancer registries are essential for improving cancer care outcomes," says Anish Patankar, Elekta's SVP & Head of Business Line Software. "By bringing together Elekta’s advanced registry software and Azra AI’s AI-driven workflow automation, we are helping hospitals advance data integrity and optimize operations. This strategic collaboration is also setting the stage for future technological advancements, benefiting oncology data specialists, hospitals and their patients."

John Marshall, CEO of Azra AI, adds: "Healthcare and cancer centers are under increasing pressure to modernize and streamline registry reporting while maintaining accuracy and compliance. Through this partnership, we are providing oncology teams with advanced tools to enhance efficiency, reduce administrative burden, and improve patient outcomes."

Elekta ONE Registry Informatics, hosted on Axis Cloud, offers a secure, web-based platform designed to optimize cancer registry workflows, improve abstracting, and ensure compliance with American College of Surgeons, North American Association of Central Cancer Registries (NAACCR), Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results (SEER), and other regulatory bodies.

Learn more about the partnership at the National Cancer Registrars Association (NCRA) Annual Education Conference, May 3-6 in Orlando at Elekta booth # 205, 207, 209.

*Elekta ONE Registry Informatics may not be available in all markets.

# # #

For further information, please contact:Mattias Thorsson, Vice President, Head of Corporate Communications

Tel: +46 70 865 8012, e-mail: Mattias.Thorsson@elekta.com  

Time zone: CET: Central European Time

Raven Canzeri, Global Director, Media Relations

Tel: +1 770-670-2524, e-mail: Raven.Canzeri@elekta.com

Time zone: ET: Eastern Time

About ElektaAs a leader in precision radiation therapy, Elekta is committed to ensuring every patient has access to the best cancer care possible. We openly collaborate with customers to advance sustainable, outcome-driven and cost-efficient solutions to meet evolving patient needs, improve lives and bring hope to everyone dealing with cancer. To us, it's personal, and our global team of 4,700 employees combine passion, science, and imagination to profoundly change cancer care. We don’t just build technology, we build hope. Elekta is headquartered in Stockholm, Sweden, with offices in more than 40 countries and listed on Nasdaq Stockholm. For more information, visit elekta.com.

data.title: Notice of Orkla's Annual General Meeting 2025
data.description:
The Annual General Meeting of Orkla ASA will be held on Thursday, 24 April 2025 at 10:00 am (CEST).

The meeting will be conducted as a digital meeting, with participation via Lumi AGM. The shareholders will be able to participate in the meeting, vote and ask questions from smartphones, tablets or desktop devices. For further information regarding attending the meeting digitally, please refer to the guide to be found at www.orkla.com.

The deadline for notice of attendance, registration of advance votes and proxies is 22 April 2025 at 11:59 p.m. (CEST) as further described in the Notice.

The Notice is posted electronically or by mail to all shareholders with known addresses.

Notice of the Annual General Meeting and appendices are enclosed and may be found at https://investors.orkla.com/events-and-presentations/annual-general-meeting/Annual-General-Meeting-2025/

The Annual General Meeting will be simultaneously translated to English, and both languages will be webcast at www.orkla.com.

Login and registration from 9:00 am (CEST).

Orkla ASA

Oslo, 3 April 2025

Ref.:

SVP Investor Relations and Communication

Annie Bersagel

Mobile: +47 98 60 36 64

E-mail: annie.bersagel@orkla.no

This information is subject to the disclosure requirements pursuant to Section 5 -12 the Norwegian Securities Trading Act

data.title: Wihlborgs signs carbon reduction certificate agreement with Öresundskraft
data.description:
Wihlborgs Fastigheter AB

Wihlborgs has signed an agreement with the Öresundskraft energy company to purchase a carbon reduction certificate as part of climate balancing Wihlborgs' own project activities. In doing so, Wihlborgs has secured access to a local balancing method to achieve its own climate targets of halving emissions from operations by 2030 and of attaining net-zero carbon emissions throughout the value chain by 2045, in line with the national targets. At the same time, Wihlborgs is participating in the facilitation of climate-neutral district heating in Helsingborg.

In 2022, Wihlborgs signed a letter of intent with the municipal energy company Öresundskraft regarding the purchase of a carbon reduction certificate. The Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) technology entails capturing carbon dioxide from flue gases generated in district heating production. The carbon dioxide is then compressed to liquid form, transported and stored permanently deep underground where it is transformed into stone. Using the same process for carbon dioxide with a biogenic origin is known as Bio-Energy Carbon Capture and Storage (BECCS) and represents a negative emission.

Wihlborgs is now following through on this letter of intent and has signed a ten-year supplier agreement using BECCS for carbon reduction certificates corresponding to 10,000 tonnes of annual emissions. Delivery is set to commence when Öresundskraft’s CO2 separation facility at the Filbornaverket plant is ready, preliminarily in 2028. Wihlborgs already has a long-standing partnership with Öresundskraft on green energy.

Carbon reduction certificates can be used to balance the climate emissions that are currently unavoidable. Wihlborgs primarily endeavours to limit its emissions through active measures both in project and in property-management operations, such as limiting the need for new materials, making active choices of necessary building material, energy optimisation and transitioning away from climate-damaging refrigerants. Wihlborgs also contributes to phasing out fossil fuels through the purchase and production of renewable energy, and by investing in solar power and energy storage. By increasing our focus on the NollCO2 certification of properties, Wihlborgs has reduced its carbon footprint from new production by 50 percent over the past five years.

“CCS enables us to offer fossil-free district heating, a major advantage as our customers are increasingly seeking climate-neutral forms of heating. Moreover, we are able to offer negative emissions to customers who wish to compensate for emissions that would otherwise be difficult to mitigate. This strengthens our role as an enabler of climate-smart energy solutions, which is both seen and appreciated by our customers. Interest is widespread and we are in talks with several potential buyers. Together, we support Helsingborg’s overall climate goals,” says Stefan Håkansson, CEO of Öresundskraft.

“The pursuit of an innovative climate initiative in the Öresund region is highly beneficial and we appreciate that Öresundskraft have assumed such a clear and forward-leaning position regarding CCS. Wihlborgs prefers to climate balance where we operate, and to leverage permanent carbon sinks close to home rather than investing in distant projects. Another benefit is that we will also be able to use climate-neutral district heating to heat our properties in Helsingborg,” says Ulrika Hallengren, CEO of Wihlborgs.   

“We have focused climate targets by 2030 that we need to find healthy balancing strategies to achieve, and as such, it is important to make an early start. Even if we continually strive to keep our emissions to a minimum, we are facing a long transition period ahead during which we will need to climate balance for emissions in our construction projects. We want to pursue this in a credible, tangible and permanent manner, which this agreement makes possible,” says Elsa Hagdahl, Head of Sustainability at Wihlborgs.

Wihlborgs Fastigheter AB (publ)

For further information, please contact:

Ulrika Hallengren, CEO, +46 40 690 57 95

Elsa Hagdahl, Head of Sustainability, +46 40 661 97 07      

Wihlborgs Fastigheter AB (publ) is a property company that focuses on commercial properties in the Öresund region. Its property portfolio is located in Malmö, Helsingborg, Lund and Copenhagen. In Malmö, Lund and Helsingborg, Wihlborgs is the leading property company. The book value of the company's properties totals SEK 59 billion, representing an annual property rental value of SEK 4.6 billion. Wihlborgs' shares are listed on the Large Cap List of Nasdaq Stockholm.

https://news.cision.com/wihlborgs-fastigheter-ab/r/wihlborgs-signs-carbon-reduction-certificate-agreement-with-oresundskraft%2Cc4130285

Press release (pdf)

Wihlborgs Öresundkraft 1

Elsa Hagdahl hållbarhetschef

data.title: Tele2 Publishes Annual and Sustainability Report 2024: 5G Expansion, Start of Transformation and Recognition as Sweden's Most Sustainable Company
data.description:
Tele2 AB

Stockholm - Tele2 AB ("Tele2") (Nasdaq Stockholm: TEL2 A and TEL2 B) today published its 2024 Annual and Sustainability Report. The report is available at www.tele2.com.

Highlights from the 2024 Annual Report

  • End-user service revenue of SEK 21,799 million increased by 3% compared to full year 2023 on an organic basis driven by growth across operations.
  • Total revenue of SEK 29,583 million increased by 2% compared to 2023 on an organic basis.
  • Underlying EBITDAaL of SEK 10,612 million increased by 2% organically compared to 2023 as end-user service revenue growth and cost savings from the Strategy Execution Program more than offset cost inflation.
  • Operating profit of SEK 5,817 million increased by 6% compared to 2023.
  • Net profit from total operations of SEK 3,870 (3,735) million and earnings per share of SEK 5.59 (5.40).
  • Continued intense rollout of 5G networks across markets, and of gigabit fixed broadband upgrades in Sweden, enabling faster, more reliable and secure services to our customers.
  • Ranked as the most sustainable company in Sweden by Time Magazine, and as the top Climate Leader 2024 in Sweden by the Financial Times.
  • Kinnevik, Tele2’s largest owner for decades, sold its shareholding to Freya Investissement, which became the largest shareholder in April 2024.
  • Kjell Johnsen stepped down as CEO and Jean Marc Harion took office as the new CEO in November.
  • The Board of Directors of Tele2 proposes an ordinary dividend of SEK 6.35 (6.90) per share, to be decided at the 2025 AGM.

-          2024 was a transformative year for Tele2. We continued our nationwide 5G rollout and reached over 90% of Sweden’s population with 5G by year end. Alongside this, we achieved major milestones in IT modernisation, all while maintaining strong operational stability. As we now shift focus to 2025, we are accelerating our transformation agenda with a sharp focus on cost control and simplification. This is about making Tele2 faster, more resilient and better equipped to capture the growth opportunities ahead, says Jean Marc Harion, President and CEO of Tele2.

Highlights from the 2024 Sustainability Report

  • Published a sustainability report in accordance with the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) for the first time, marking a new level of transparency and alignment with EU regulations.
  • Ranked number two among Europe’s top 500 climate leaders by The Financial Times and named Sweden’s most sustainable company by Time Magazine​.
  • Ranked number fifty-eight globally for gender equality by Equileap, placing Tele2 in the global top 100 out of more than 4,000 evaluated companies​.
  • Blocked over 7 million attempts to access child sexual abuse material in 2024 in Sweden and the Baltics, a 40% increase compared to 2023​.
  • Maintaining a top rating (A) from CDP for leadership in corporate transparency and performance on climate change and recognised by the global environmental non-profit organisation for its climate leadership​.
  • Over 1 million tonnes CO2-eq greenhouse gas emissions estimated to have been avoided by customers, enabled by Tele2’s products and services​.
  • 96% reduction of Scope 1 and 2 emissions compared to the base year 2019, surpassing the 2025 target of 90% reduction​
  • Collected 64,000 mobile phones through its trade-in program in the past year, a significant step towards the goal of having one third of all sold phones returned for recycling and reuse by 2030.

-          Our sustainability efforts continue to gain global recognition, and in 2024 we were proud to be named Sweden’s most sustainable company by Time Magazine and ranked number 2 on the Financial Times list of Europe’s Climate Leaders. These acknowledgements reflect our long-term commitment to integrating sustainability into our core business. Whether it’s blocking over seven million attempts to access child sexual abuse material, reducing our Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 96% since 2019, or helping our customers avoid over one million tonnes of CO₂ emissions through our products and services — we are committed to being a driving force in enabling a more sustainable society, Jean Marc Harion concludes.

For more information, please contact:

Fredrik Hallstan, Head of External Communications Phone: +46 761 15 38 30

Stefan Billing, Head of Investor Relations, Phone: +46 701 66 33 10

 

This is information that Tele2 AB (publ) is obliged to make public pursuant to the Securities Markets Act. The information was submitted for publication at 07:30 CET on 3 April 2025.

 

About Tele2

Tele2 is a leading telco with the purpose to enable a society of unlimited possibilities. Through our networks and services - ranging from mobile and fixed connectivity, telephony and data network services to TV, streaming and global IoT solutions - we are deeply connected to every aspect of today's digital lifestyle. By making our infrastructure more powerful, reliable and sustainable, we enable society to become that as well. Tele2 was founded in 1993 and is listed on Nasdaq Stockholm. In 2024, Tele2 generated revenue of SEK 30 billion and reported an underlying EBITDAaL of SEK 11 billion. For latest news and definitions of measures, please see our homepage www.tele2.com

https://news.cision.com/tele2-ab/r/tele2-publishes-annual-and-sustainability-report-2024--5g-expansion--start-of-transformation-and-rec%2Cc4130047

Tele2 Annual and Sustainability Report 2024

Press release Tele2 annual and sustainability report 2024

Jean Marc Harion - CEO Tele2

data.title: Techstep ASA enters an extensive agreement with LKAB
data.description:
Techstep ASA, a leading mobile and circular technology company, is excited toannounce that it has entered into an extensive agreement with LKAB, one ofSweden's most historically significant industrial companies. With a legacydating back to the 1800s, LKAB is renowned for its continuous focus onmodernization and efficiency.

Under the agreement, Techstep will be responsible for managing LKAB's growingmobility initiatives in a secure, efficient, and sustainable manner. Techstepwill deliver, manage and support their entire mobile estate with mobiledevices,experience and security management. This agreement aligns perfectly withTechstep's strategy to scale and help customers succeed with mobile ways ofworking by delivering end-to-end services that cover the entire lifecycle ofmobility, from onboarding to offboarding, ensuring that business-criticalmobility meets customer needs and expectations.

"We are honored and delighted to step into this role and continue building ourpartnership with an innovative and forward-thinking mobile first company likeLKAB. We are proud to be given the responsibility of delivering comprehensivemobility management services for LKAB" says Morten Meier, CEO of Techstep.

The agreement initially spans three years and covers LKAB's entire mobileestate.

For more information, please contact:Morten Meier, CEO Techstep ASA    +47 97057717   morten.meier@techstep.io

About LKAB

LKAB is an international mining and minerals group that offers sustainableironore, minerals and special products. We are committed to developing carbon-freeprocesses and products by 2045, leading the transformation of the iron andsteelindustry. Since 1890 we have developed through unique innovations andtechnological solutions and are driven forward by almost 5,200 employees in 12countries. In 2024, the LKAB group had sales of about SEK 33 billion. lkab.com

About Techstep ASA

Techstep is a mobile & circular technology company, enabling organisations toperform smartly, securely, and sustainably by combining mobile devices,softwareand expertise to meet customers' business and ESG goals. We are a leadingprovider of managed mobility services in Europe, serving more than 2,100customers in Europe with an annual revenue of NOK 1.1 billion in 2024. Thecompany is listed on the Oslo Stock Exchange under the ticker TECH. To learnmore, please visit www.techstep.io.

data.title: Skanska conducts groundworks for Stockholm Exergy's new Beccs facility in Stockholm, Sweden, for about SEK 990M
data.description:
Skanska

Skanska has signed a contract with Stockholm Exergi to lay the foundations and build a quay for the upcoming Beccs Stockholm facility, one of the world's largest facilities for capturing and permanently storing biogenic carbon dioxide, in Stockholm, Sweden. The contract is worth about SEK 990M, which will be included in the order bookings for Skanska Sweden for the second quarter of 2025.

The existing agreement between the parties for Phase 1 is now complemented by a contract for Phase 2, which includes foundation, ground, and concrete work for the Beccs building as well as a new quay facility.

Stockholm Exergi's facility, Beccs Stockholm, will be built in Energihamnen in Värtan. The nearby Värtaverket already produces sustainable heat and electricity from residues from the forest and sawmill industries. Now, the capture and storage of biogenic carbon dioxide are also being added. The Beccs facility will annually capture and store approximately 800,000 tons of CO2 from flue gases generated by Stockholm Exergi's biomass power plant Värtaverket.

Work on the new Beccs facility starts immediately and is planned to be completed during 2028.

Bio-CCS (Bio Energy Carbon Capture and Storage) is a technology that captures biogenic carbon dioxide before it reaches the atmosphere to then be permanently stored in bedrock. The construction of the facility is an important part of the City of Stockholm's goal to become a climate-positive area by 2030. Beccs Stockholm is made possible through a combination of support from the EU's innovation fund, state aid, and private purchases of certificates for negative emissions from companies with high climate ambitions.

For further information please contact:

Andreas Joons, Press Officer, Skanska Group, tel +46 (0)10 449 04 94

Direct line for media, tel +46 (0)10 448 88 99

This and previous releases can also be found at www.skanska.com.                 

Skanska Group uses its knowledge and foresight to shape the way people live, work, and connect. We are one of the world's largest construction and project development companies, with 2024 revenue totaling SEK 177 billion. We operate across select markets in the Nordics, Europe and USA. Together with our customers and the collective expertise of our approximately 26,300 teammates, we create innovative and sustainable solutions that support healthy living beyond our lifetime.

data.title: Market Chatter: Tesla Logs 12% Fall in March EV Sales in China Amid Fierce Competition
data.description:
US carmaker Tesla's electric vehicle sales in China slumped in March amid tight competition in the sector, Reuters reported Wednesday.

Tesla saw sales of China-made EVs decline 12% year over year to 78,828 units in March, according to the report. Deliveries of China-made Model 3 and Model Y vehicles rose 157% % from February.

In comparison, BYD logged a 23% rise to 371,419 units in passenger vehicle sales in March, Reuters wrote.

Tesla is planning to launch a cheaper Model Y variant from Shanghai in 2026 to counter its competitors, according to the report.

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0123 ET - Trade organization spiritsEurope says it supports any efforts made by the European Union to reach a negotiated solution with the U.S. as the spirits sector braces for the tariffs announced by President Trump. The industry is highly integrated across the Atlantic, spiritsEurope says, making the sector particularly vulnerable to tariffs. The association, which represents the sector in the EU, says it is prepared to contribute with a proposal to improve the fortunes of the industry on both sides of the Atlantic."We want to ensure a return to an environment that supports continued growth for both EU and U.S. producers," the association adds. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0104 ET - Spain will keep fighting for an open world, premier Pedro Sanchez insists as the EU braces for 20% tariffs on its exports to the U.S., the bloc's most important trading partner. The trade duties, part of a major package set out Wednesday by President Trump, could hamstring exporters and depress Europe's growth. "Spain will protect its companies and its workers and will continue to back openness in the world," Sanchez says on X following a meeting with employer and worker associations.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

2232 ET - The sweeping Trump tariffs could pose higher-than-expected risks to China's exports, says Pinpoint Asset Management chief economist Zhiwei Zhang in a note. The high tariffs on Asean countries, such as Vietnam and Cambodia, could weigh on China's exports to these countries, he says. Supply chains in East Asia are vertically integrated as China's exports of intermediate goods are crucial for these countries' exports, he says. "Retaliation from China on the U.S. tariffs is likely coming," he says. Zhang expects Beijing to push up fiscal spending and cut interest rate and reserve requirement ratio to offset the damage to China's exports, he says. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2100 ET - NSW Farmers, which represents producers in Australia's most populous state of New South Wales, urged Australia to invest in new markets after the U.S. imposed a 10% reciprocal tariff on Australian imports. Trump mentioned Australian beef in his speech announcing the tariffs, saying that Australia doesn't import American beef but that the U.S. imports a lot of Australian beef. NSW Farmers say Australian produce is in demand in China, Korea and Japan, and there are growth opportunities in the Middle East and North Africa. The group, however, stood by the Australian government's decision not to retaliate, with NSW Farmers President Xavier Martin saying that "a tit-for-tat approach with the U.S. serves no purpose." (mike.cherney@wsj.com; @Mike_Cherney)

2033 ET - AUD​/USD ​has ranged through a high ​of 0.6341​ to a low of low of 0.6226​ since U.S. President Trump announced a sweeping new tariff regime on his country's trading partners. The pair now trades at 0.6259. It amounts to only a modest net decline for the ​AUD, with Australia escaping the tariff hike relatively lightly, and with lots of bad news already priced into the currency​, says Sean Callow, currency analyst at InTouch Capital Markets. Traders will be bracing for China's response​ through the USD/CNY fix, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

2010 ET - Copper has escaped a tariff on sales into the U.S.--at least for now. Copper and bullion, among some other products, will not be subject to President Trump's reciprocal tariff plan, according to the White House. Still, that could be because it will likely face its own tariff later, says AMP's Shane Oliver. Last month, Trump threatened to impose a duty of as much as 25% on imported copper. The U.S. mostly imports copper from Chile, Canada and Peru. Steel and aluminum, already subject to a 25% tariff, were also on the exemption list. LME 3-month copper falls by 1.2%, amid concerns about the impact of tariffs on global growth. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1936 ET - President Trump's reciprocal tariff on major appliance manufacturing countries, including China, Vietnam, and Thailand, is likely to lead to retailers raising platform fees by another 30%, according to Jefferies analysts in a note. Unlike China, the U.S. appliance market is dominated by large chain stores such as Home Depot, Best Buy, and Amazon. The note states that with these platforms increasing their fees, brands' operations in the U.S. would become largely unprofitable. (venkat.pr@wsj.com)

1933 ET - Trump's reciprocal tariff on major textile and footwear manufacturing nations will place original equipment manufacturers under significant margin and price pressure over the next 3-5 years, Jefferies says in a note. Vietnam is likely to lose its status as the leading exporter of sporting goods to the U.S. At the same time, new capacity is expected to be added in low-cost countries like Indonesia, India, and in Africa. Surging capital expenditures and margin pressure may result in cash burn for the OEMs, it adds. (venkat.pr@wsj.com)

1932 ET - President Trump's decision to impose 10% tariffs on imports into the U.S. from Australia is a "conspicuously unfriendly act", says Saul Eslake, a former chief economist at ANZ, and later Bank of America Merrill Lynch, Australia. Australia is one of very few countries with which the U.S. runs a trade surplus and provides the U.S. with valuable services by hosting vital military and intelligence-gathering facilities and bases, he says. Trump's America is clearly no friend of Australia, "and the sooner we wake up to that, the better," he adds. Ordinary Australians should think twice about buying American-made products, or taking holidays in the U.S. If ordinary people around the world all did the same, that would send a much more powerful message, Eslake says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1918 ET - President Trump's reciprocal tariffs could hurt Apple's gross margin, three analysts at Citi Research say in a research report. Assuming that Apple can't get exempted this time and that Apple gets hit by the U.S.'s accumulative 54% tariffs on China and doesn't pass this through, there could be around an estimated 9% negative effect on the company's total gross margin, the analysts say. Citi estimates Apple presently has more than 90% of its manufacturing in China. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1912 ET - President Trump's tariff "blow (is) bigger than we expected," Capital Economics' Neil Shearing and Paul Ashworth say in a research report. Capital Economics' calculations imply an import-weighted average tariff of 19.1%, the economists say. If reciprocal tariffs stack on top of earlier tariff increases on China and product-specific tariffs on steel, aluminum and autos, this means effective tariff rate on all imports will rise to around 26% from 2.3% last year, the economists estimate. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1908 ET - Desjardins Group economist Royce Mendes says President Trump's new tariff policy, as it applies to Canada, reinforces the firm's view that the Bank of Canada will remain on hold on April 16, or its next rate-policy announcement. Canada and Mexico will face no new duties, other than what was previously announced prior to Wednesday. Mendes says this gives Canada and Mexico "some short-term advantage" over other countries. As a result there is some scope for the two countries to take market share from other U.S. trading partners. Mendes warns that while there remain unresolved trade irritants between the U.S. and Canada, traders might interpret the Liberation Day moves as a starting point for negotiations on a new North American trade pact. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)
